item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The skip can control "social bonds" of people who know about it. It's not specified exactly what this means but it's clearly not good.

The containment is to surround it with a dozen people who have a random social structure, random knowledge of what's going on, and random goals (none of which actually matter). It's not said, but presumably the knowledge of this entity was erased from everyone else's mind. Each week the participants are reset and re-randomized, and some of them are swapped out with fresh personnel. This limits the changes the entity can make and probably also keeps it confused.

You (the reader) are starting a new shift. It may or may not be your first.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: reading this a few months later, with a different perspective on novotes, i now am deciding to -1 this article. weirdly enough i would +1 this if it were a -J.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Bloody amazing. In my opinion, changing the text on refresh wouldn't significantly improve the article, and the amount of randomness feels exactly right as is. +1
Thanks, everyone!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Me and the boys playing Madlibs

Great concept, cool format screw, probably missing something, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like the idea of hypnotizing the readers before they read access the file.
+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
i agree with two of the above reviewers:

-the format screw became a bit stale halfway through
-i was hoping to get new alterations when i refreshed

overall this is a no vote for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …I have no idea what is going on here and I love it. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like how you handled how to keep people from “knowing the existence” of the SCP. Some of them just blatantly tell you “ay, you know this thing exists and people who know this thing exist will die in 15 seconds ok bye gg.” You made it possible to tell this SCP without dying and that’s what I love about it.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: …yeah… my brain is scrambled eggs and I taste like a fire extinguisher now, but the SCP was actually pretty damn excellent

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Like it. I'd love it more if I had been correct in guessing that if i refreshed the page, I'd get new alterations.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I felt that the format screw became a little dry about half-way through the article, and I wasn't exactly getting anything out of the article itself because I felt my interest waning.

Maybe it's just my personal taste, but I can't say I'd upvote this article in its current state.

It's a no-vote from me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very fun. Just about the right length for the concept, I think. +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I don't think I have much to say about this one beyond that it's fucking nuts in all the right ways.

+1

EDIT: Bonus cred on keeping the format screw working the whole way through without it becoming monotonous

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I don't think I understand it fully, but I think I like it!

item.title: The real horror was navigating social situations the whole time
item.content:encoded:
This is a weird one, even for me. It's essentially an attempt to make an article where the narrative is wholly in the containment procedures; I did a similar thing with SCP-5790 — [DATA KILLED], and funnily enough this was also a potential contribution for the SCP-5000 contest. In the end, I think my choice made sense — there's really not enough of a mystery theme here to justify it.

Image source is in the caption.

EDIT: For those of you disappointed by the text not changing: I feel you. I had the same idea. I just didn't feel like the added expense of writing the whole thing to be javascript compatible would pay off that much story-wise; I also don't like the idea that people would upvote simply based on the effort of a gimmick. This feels like a much more concept-oriented piece, rather than just a flex of my programming abilities. Plus, it's a well-known fact that well-constructed fake randomness always feels more random than actual randomness :P
